name: Data Visualization: Charting Well
description: Choose the right chart and make it honest: the perceptual ranking of visual encodings, chart-type selection with real figures to name on sight, axis and log-scale rules, colorblind-safe color, error bars done right, and the classic graphics from Playfair to Minard.

fields.front: Four datasets can share identical means, variances, and correlations yet look totally different when plotted. What habit does this force on you?
fields.back: Always plot the raw data before trusting summary statistics.
fields.extra: Anscombe's quartet (1973) is the canonical demonstration; the Datasaurus Dozen is its modern remake.

fields.front: Name this famous set of four datasets.
fields.back: Anscombe's quartet.
fields.extra: Constructed by Francis Anscombe in 1973: same means, variances, correlations, and regression line, four completely different shapes.

fields.front: What is the Datasaurus Dozen?
fields.back: A modern Anscombe-style collection of datasets with near-identical summary statistics, one shaped like a dinosaur.
fields.extra: Matejka and Fitzmaurice (2017) evolved each dataset by tiny random nudges that preserved the statistics while reshaping the plot.

fields.front: In Cleveland and McGill's accuracy ranking of visual encodings, what sits at the top?
fields.back: Position along a common scale.
fields.extra: Their 1984 experiments measured how accurately people decode each encoding. This is why scatter plots, dot plots, and aligned bars are the workhorses of quantitative graphics.

fields.front: In the Cleveland-McGill ranking, where does color shading fall?
fields.back: At the bottom: the least accurately judged encoding.
fields.extra: Rough order: position, then length, then angle/slope, then area, then volume, then color. Color is fine for overview patterns (heatmaps), wrong for exact value reading.

fields.front: Why do bar charts beat pie charts for comparing values?
fields.back: Bars use length and position judgments, which humans make more accurately than the angle judgments pies require.
fields.extra: If the reader must rank or compare parts, use bars; keep pies for a rough sense of one dominant share.

fields.front: What is Tufte's data-ink ratio?
fields.back: The proportion of a graphic's ink that displays actual data.
fields.extra: Maximize it: every gridline, border, and effect that carries no information competes with the data. Erase until removing more would lose meaning.

fields.front: Tufte's word for chart decoration that carries no data?
fields.extra: Moire patterns, heavy gridlines, 3D effects, clip art. The cure is not minimalism for its own sake but spending ink on data.

fields.text: Tufte's lie factor = (size of the effect shown in the graphic) / (size of the effect in the {{c1::data}}).
fields.extra: An honest chart scores close to 1. Truncated bar axes are the classic way to inflate it.

fields.front: What does Tufte call a grid of little charts with identical scales, one panel per condition?
fields.back: Small multiples.
fields.extra: Also called trellis, facet, or lattice plots. Once readers parse one panel they can read all of them; comparison happens by eye movement, not memory.

fields.front: This 1878 photo sequence is often cited as an ancestor of which figure technique?
fields.back: Small multiples.
fields.extra: Eadweard Muybridge's The Horse in Motion: same frame, same scale, one variable (time) changing per panel.

fields.front: Default chart for comparing one numeric value across a handful of categories?
fields.back: A bar chart.
fields.extra: Position on a common scale with a zero baseline. Sort by value when the categories have no natural order.

fields.front: Default chart for a value changing over continuous time?
fields.back: A line chart.
fields.extra: The slope carries the meaning. Use points instead of a connecting line when observations are sparse or irregular.

fields.front: Name this chart type.
fields.back: A line chart (time series).
fields.extra: The Keeling curve: monthly CO2 at Mauna Loa. One line encodes both the seasonal sawtooth and the long-term trend.

fields.front: Default chart for the relationship between two continuous variables?
fields.back: A scatter plot.
fields.extra: Each point is one observation. Add a trend line only after showing the points; the line alone hides the spread.

fields.front: Name this chart type.
fields.back: A scatter plot.
fields.extra: Two continuous axes, one point per observation; here a process input against a quality metric, with a clear negative trend.

fields.front: In a scatter plot of 100k points, most points hide each other. What is this problem called?
fields.back: Overplotting.
fields.extra: Fixes: transparency (alpha), smaller markers, hex-binning or 2D density, or plotting a sample.

fields.front: Default chart for the distribution of a single numeric variable?
fields.back: A histogram.
fields.extra: Bin the values, count per bin. Always try more than one bin width; the apparent shape can change.

fields.front: Name this chart type.
fields.back: A histogram.
fields.extra: Heights of 31 black cherry trees. Bars touch because the x-axis is continuous; a categorical bar chart keeps gaps.

fields.front: What does a kernel density estimate (KDE) show?
fields.back: A smoothed continuous estimate of a variable's distribution.
fields.extra: It replaces histogram bins with a bandwidth choice; too wide oversmooths, too narrow shows noise.

fields.front: The smooth curve drawn over this histogram is called what?
fields.back: A kernel density estimate (KDE).
fields.extra: Same data, two views: the histogram's shape depends on bin edges, the KDE's on bandwidth.

fields.front: In a box plot, what does the box itself span?
fields.back: The interquartile range: the middle 50% of the data.
fields.extra: Bottom edge Q1, top edge Q3. Whiskers typically reach the most extreme points within 1.5 IQR; dots beyond are flagged as outliers.

fields.front: In a box plot, the line inside the box marks what?
fields.back: The median.
fields.extra: Not the mean. Some styles add the mean as a separate dot or a notch.

fields.front: Name this chart type.
fields.back: A box plot (box-and-whisker plot).
fields.extra: Michelson's speed-of-light measurements, one box per experiment; circles are outliers beyond the whiskers, the red line the modern true value.

fields.front: What can a violin plot show that a box plot structurally cannot?
fields.back: The full shape of the distribution, including bimodality.
fields.extra: A box plot collapses everything to five numbers; two very different distributions can share one identical box.

fields.front: Name this chart type.
fields.back: A violin plot.
fields.extra: A mirrored density estimate per group; the width at each height is the density there.

fields.front: When does a heatmap beat grouped bars?
fields.back: When the data is a wide two-way grid, where color patterns read better than dozens of bars.
fields.extra: Condition x training stage, layer x head, month x hour. Readers get patterns and outliers, not precise values; print numbers in the cells if exact values matter.

fields.front: Name this chart type.
fields.back: A (clustered) heatmap.
fields.extra: Gene expression data: rows are samples, columns are genes, color is the value; the tree diagrams (dendrograms) on the edges show hierarchical clustering.

fields.front: Eight time series tangle into spaghetti on one panel. What is the standard fix?
fields.back: Small multiples: one small panel per series, with shared axes.
fields.extra: Alternative: repeat the full spaghetti in gray in every panel and highlight one series per panel for context.

fields.front: In a stacked bar chart, why is only the bottom segment easy to compare across bars?
fields.back: Only the bottom segment starts from a common baseline; every other segment floats.
fields.extra: If comparisons of a middle category matter, unstack into grouped bars or small multiples.

fields.front: What is a forest plot for?
fields.back: Showing many effect estimates with their confidence intervals against one common axis.
fields.extra: Home turf is meta-analysis: one row per study or subgroup, a vertical no-effect line, a diamond for the pooled estimate.

fields.front: Name this chart type.
fields.back: A forest plot.
fields.extra: Squares are individual study estimates (sized by weight), horizontal lines their confidence intervals, the diamond the pooled estimate.

fields.front: What question does a Q-Q plot answer?
fields.back: Whether a sample follows a given theoretical distribution.
fields.extra: It plots sample quantiles against theoretical quantiles; systematic curvature means skew or unusual tails.

fields.front: In this Q-Q plot the points hug the diagonal. Conclusion?
fields.back: The sample is consistent with the theoretical (here normal) distribution.
fields.extra: An S-shape means the tails are too heavy or too light; a bowed arc means skew.

fields.front: What kind of map is this?
fields.back: A choropleth: regions shaded by a data value.
fields.extra: The 2004 US election by county, on a blue-purple-red diverging scale centered at 50/50.

fields.front: Why should a choropleth shade rates rather than raw counts?
fields.back: Raw counts mostly redraw the population map: populous regions win everything.
fields.extra: Deaths per 100k, not deaths. Also beware large but empty areas dominating the eye.

fields.front: Why must a bar chart's value axis start at zero?
fields.back: Bars encode value by length, so a cut axis falsifies the ratios between bars.
fields.extra: A bar twice as long must mean twice the value; truncation breaks exactly that.

fields.front: E's bar towers over A's, but E is only about 4% larger. What is the trick?
fields.back: A truncated y-axis: it starts at 9100 instead of zero.
fields.extra: The single most common misleading-graph device; Tufte's lie factor far above 1.

fields.front: Does a line chart's axis have to include zero?
fields.back: No: lines encode change by position and slope, so the axis should frame the data's range.
fields.extra: The bar-chart zero rule does not transfer. Forcing zero can flatten the line and hide the very signal you are plotting.

fields.front: Your values run from about 1e2 to 1e6. What scale does the chart need?
fields.back: A logarithmic scale.
fields.extra: On a linear axis the small values flatten into the floor. Loss curves, compute budgets, and latencies usually live on log axes.

fields.front: A quantity plots as a straight line on a log y-axis. What kind of growth is that?
fields.back: Exponential growth (constant percent change).
fields.extra: Straight on log-log axes instead means a power law, the shape of scaling-law plots.

fields.front: Equal steps along a log axis represent what?
fields.back: Equal multiplicative factors.
fields.extra: 1, 10, 100 sit evenly spaced. Label ticks with actual values, not bare exponents, for non-technical readers.

fields.front: Why are dual y-axis charts distrusted?
fields.back: The two scales are chosen independently, so how the lines track or cross is an artifact of that choice.
fields.extra: Fixes: index both series to 100 at a common point, use small multiples, or a connected scatter.

fields.front: Palette type for ordered values running low to high?
fields.extra: One hue ramping in lightness: light = low, dark = high (or the reverse, applied consistently).

fields.front: Palette type for values with a meaningful midpoint, like zero change?
fields.extra: Two hues meeting at a neutral center. The purple-counties election map is one: blue to purple to red around 50/50.

fields.front: Palette type for unordered categories?
fields.extra: Distinct hues of similar lightness. Beyond about 6-8 categories no palette saves you; group or facet instead.

fields.front: Why was the rainbow (jet) colormap banished from serious plotting?
fields.back: Its lightness rises and falls unevenly, creating false boundaries and hiding real structure.
fields.extra: It is also unreadable in grayscale and hostile to colorblind readers.

fields.front: Name the perceptually uniform, colorblind-safe colormap family that replaced jet as the scientific default.
fields.back: Viridis (with siblings magma, plasma, and inferno).
fields.extra: Designed for matplotlib; lightness increases monotonically, so equal data steps read as equal color steps and grayscale printing still works.

fields.text: About {{c1::1 in 12}} men have red-green color vision deficiency.
fields.extra: Roughly 8% of men and 0.5% of women. In any decent-sized audience, someone cannot see your red-vs-green distinction.

fields.front: Which color pair must never be the only thing distinguishing two series?
fields.back: Red and green.
fields.extra: The most common color vision deficiency collapses them. Blue-orange is the safe contrast pair; better still, add a second channel.

fields.front: How do you make a multi-series chart survive both grayscale printing and colorblind readers?
fields.back: Encode the series redundantly: color plus marker shape, line style, or direct labels.
fields.extra: Cheap test: print the figure in grayscale; if you cannot tell the series apart, neither can part of your audience.

fields.front: An error bar means nothing until the caption says what?
fields.back: What the bar shows: SD, SEM, or a confidence interval.
fields.extra: The three differ by large factors on the same data; reviewers check for this first.

fields.front: Why do SEM error bars flatter your results compared to SD bars?
fields.back: SEM = SD / sqrt(n), so SEM bars are always narrower.
fields.extra: SD shows spread of the data, SEM/CI show uncertainty about the mean. Choose for the claim you are making, then say which you chose.

fields.front: Your figure shows two group means as bars with error whiskers (a 'dynamite plot'). What should you usually show instead?
fields.back: The underlying points or distributions, because means with whiskers hide spread, skew, and outliers.
fields.extra: Overlay jittered raw points, or switch to box/violin plots when n is large.

fields.front: Besides defining the error bars, what number must a results-figure caption state?
fields.back: The n: what was averaged, over how many seeds, examples, or runs.
fields.extra: 'Mean over 5 seeds, shaded region = 95% CI' is one clause and answers the reviewer's first question.

fields.front: Many readers see only your figures. What does that imply about captions?
fields.back: Each caption must stand alone as a self-contained summary of the figure.
fields.extra: Skimming reviewers jump from abstract to figures. Say what is plotted, how it was computed, and what to conclude, without leaning on the body text.

fields.front: What beats a legend when a chart has only a few lines?
fields.back: Direct labels: series names placed right next to their lines.
fields.extra: Kills the eye ping-pong between legend and data, and survives grayscale and colorblindness for free.

fields.front: With no natural category order, how should bars be sorted?
fields.back: By value.
fields.extra: Alphabetical order is a random permutation of your data; sorted bars turn the chart into a ranking for free.

fields.front: Why export paper figures as PDF/SVG rather than PNG?
fields.back: Vector graphics stay sharp at every zoom and print size.
fields.extra: Exception: rasterize a million-point scatter layer inside an otherwise-vector figure, or the PDF grinds viewers to a halt.

fields.front: Name the chart crime committed here.
fields.back: A 3D exploded pie chart: perspective distorts every slice's apparent area.
fields.extra: Foreground slices gain apparent area from the visible rim and tilt. If it must be a pie, it must at least be flat.

fields.front: When is a pie chart actually defensible?
fields.back: For parts of a whole with very few slices, where the point is one rough dominant share.
fields.extra: 'About two-thirds of traffic' is pie territory; any ranking or precise comparison belongs to bars.

fields.front: What does this famous 1869 flow map depict?
fields.back: Napoleon's 1812 march on Moscow and the retreat.
fields.extra: By Charles Joseph Minard. Tufte called it possibly the best statistical graphic ever drawn: it encodes army size, route, direction, and temperature in one image.

fields.front: In Minard's Napoleon map, what does the width of the band encode?
fields.back: The size of the surviving army.
fields.extra: About 422,000 men crossing into Russia shrink to about 10,000 returning; tan is the advance, black the retreat, with temperature plotted beneath.

fields.front: Whose 1854 map is this, plotting cholera deaths as stacked marks by address?
fields.back: John Snow's Broad Street outbreak map.
fields.extra: Deaths cluster around the Broad Street water pump, evidence for waterborne transmission; a founding artifact of epidemiology.

fields.front: Who created this 'rose diagram' of army mortality?
fields.back: Florence Nightingale.
fields.extra: Her 1858 polar-area ('coxcomb') diagram showed preventable disease killed far more soldiers than wounds, and helped push Parliament into sanitary reform.

fields.front: This 1786 trade-balance chart comes from the inventor of the line, area, and bar chart. Who?
fields.back: William Playfair.
fields.extra: From his Commercial and Political Atlas; he added the pie chart in 1801. Nearly every standard chart form is one Scotsman's invention.
